questions.question: The moon landing occurred on July 20, 1969. Which astronaut remained in orbit while the others walked on the moon?
questions.explanation: Michael Collins piloted the Columbia command module and orbited the Moon while Neil Armstrong and Buzz Aldrin landed in the Eagle lunar module. Collins is sometimes called 'the forgotten astronaut,' but he described his solo orbits of the moon — sometimes on the far side with no contact with Earth — as peaceful.
questions.memory_hook: Collins orbited alone — the most isolated human in history, 250,000 miles from Earth.

questions.question: The 'Panama Canal' connects which two oceans?
questions.explanation: The Panama Canal connects the Atlantic Ocean (via the Caribbean Sea) to the Pacific Ocean, cutting through Panama. It opened in 1914 and reduced the voyage between the Atlantic and Pacific by around 12,000 miles, eliminating the need to go around the southern tip of South America.
questions.memory_hook: Panama Canal: Atlantic to Pacific. Cut through Central America, saved 12,000 miles.

questions.question: In American football, how many points is a field goal worth?
questions.explanation: A field goal in American football is worth 3 points. It is scored by kicking the ball through the upright goalposts. The longest successful field goal in NFL history was 66 yards, kicked by Justin Tucker of the Baltimore Ravens in 2021.
questions.memory_hook: Field goal = 3. Touchdown = 6. One kicker, half as many points.

questions.question: The Olympic torch relay tradition, where a flame is carried from Greece to the host city, was introduced at which Olympic Games?
questions.explanation: The Olympic torch relay was introduced at the 1936 Berlin Games, organized under Nazi Germany. A flame was lit in Olympia, Greece and carried by relay runners to Berlin — a tradition that has continued at every Summer Olympics since.
questions.memory_hook: 1936 Berlin — the torch relay began, and never stopped.

questions.question: What sport uses the term 'en garde'?
questions.explanation: 'En garde' is a French phrase meaning 'on guard' and is the starting command in fencing bouts. It instructs fencers to assume their ready positions. The phrase has entered common usage to mean 'prepare to defend yourself' or as a warning that a challenge is coming.
questions.memory_hook: 'En garde!' — French for 'get ready to be stabbed.'

questions.question: Who played the title character in 'Black Panther: Wakanda Forever'?
questions.explanation: Letitia Wright took center stage in Black Panther: Wakanda Forever (2022) as Shuri, who dons the Black Panther mantle after her brother T'Challa's death (reflecting Chadwick Boseman's real-life passing). The film grossed over $859 million worldwide and paid tribute to Boseman throughout.
questions.memory_hook: Shuri (Wright) became Wakanda's new protector after Boseman's T'Challa was mourned on-screen.

questions.question: What year was the original 'Ghostbusters' released?
questions.explanation: Ghostbusters was released on June 8, 1984, starring Bill Murray, Dan Aykroyd, Harold Ramis, and Ernie Hudson. Written by Aykroyd and Ramis, the film was a massive commercial success and spawned a franchise including sequels, animated series, and a 2021 continuation.
questions.memory_hook: 1984: Ghostbusters. Who ya gonna call? Answer: the box office — it was a hit.

questions.question: What was the first James Bond film?
questions.explanation: Dr. No (1962) was the first James Bond film, starring Sean Connery as 007. It was based on Ian Fleming's 1958 novel and set in Jamaica. The film introduced the Bond formula: exotic locations, beautiful women, a memorable villain, and sophisticated gadgets. Dr. No grossed 60 times its budget.
questions.memory_hook: Bond began with 'No' — Dr. No, 1962, Connery in Jamaica.

questions.question: The Holocaust was the systematic genocide of which group, among others?
options: Jewish people and other targeted groups
questions.answer: Jewish people and other targeted groups
questions.explanation: The Holocaust was the systematic, state-sponsored persecution and murder of six million Jewish people by the Nazi regime and its collaborators. Other targeted groups included Roma, people with disabilities, LGBTQ+ individuals, political opponents, and Jehovah's Witnesses. In total, an estimated 11 million people were killed.
questions.memory_hook: Six million Jewish lives lost. Eleven million total. Never forget.

questions.question: Who was the last tsar of Russia?
questions.explanation: Nicholas II was the last Emperor of Russia, ruling from 1894 until the Russian Revolution of 1917 forced his abdication. He and his family were executed by Bolshevik forces on July 17, 1918. His reign was marked by political repression and Russia's devastating involvement in World War I.
questions.memory_hook: Nicholas II — the last tsar. Russia's final emperor, executed by his own revolution.

questions.question: Which river flows through the most countries in Europe?
questions.explanation: The Danube flows through or forms the border of 10 countries: Germany, Austria, Slovakia, Hungary, Croatia, Serbia, Bulgaria, Romania, Moldova, and Ukraine before emptying into the Black Sea. At 2,860 km, it is Europe's second longest river after the Volga. Vienna, Bratislava, Budapest, and Belgrade all sit on the Danube.
questions.memory_hook: Danube = 10 countries. No other river in the world touches as many nations. Four capital cities sit on its banks. Johann Strauss wrote a famous waltz about it.

questions.question: How many elements are currently on the periodic table?
questions.explanation: The periodic table currently contains 118 confirmed elements. Elements 1-94 (hydrogen through plutonium) occur naturally on Earth; elements 95-118 are synthetic and were created in laboratories. Element 118, Oganesson, was first synthesized in 2002 and is so unstable it only exists for milliseconds.
questions.memory_hook: 118 elements on the periodic table. 94 occur in nature, 24 are man-made. Every substance in the universe is made from combinations of just 118 basic building blocks.
